one_line_takeaway: A non-security, engineering-focused deep watermarking paper: it improves screen-capture-robust image watermarking via a physically-motivated Moire noise simulator, a JND-guided perceptual loss, and automatic segmentation/anti-cropping localization, achieving 127-bit payloads at ~31 dB PSNR with 1-3% bit error rates across devices/angles/distances -- relevant to copyright/leak-tracing engineering, not to AI-security threat models.
key_finding: On the full pipeline (Table 8/main results), the method embeds 127-bit payloads at PSNR 30.94 dB / SSIM 0.94 (ablation NET3 setting) and, in the SOTA comparison, PSNR 31.77 dB / SSIM 0.91 / LPIPS 0.05 (Table 10), with BER of 1.09%-1.42% at the standard 40 cm frontal condition and 1-3% BER across most tested distances/angles/devices (Table 8), degrading to 11.65% only at the extreme 200 cm / Redmi K30 condition. Ablation (Table 4) shows the JND loss monotonically improves PSNR/SSIM/LPIPS as eta decreases, at the cost of rising BER (e.g. NET2 eta=1: 22.86 dB/0.772/0.109/0.14% BER vs eta=0.2: 25.00 dB/0.845/0.075/0.49% BER); the adopted 2-channel NET3 (eta=1) configuration reaches 30.94 dB/0.942 SSIM/0.048 LPIPS/2.86% BER. Moire-simulation ablation (Table 5) shows NET4 (with Moire sim) beats NET5 (without) at every tested angle from -75 deg to +75 deg, by 0.10-1.15 percentage points BER, with the largest gains at extreme angles (-75 deg: 7.46% vs 8.27%; +75 deg: 11.13% vs 12.05%). Localization ablation (Table 6) shows unlocalized decoding gives near-random BER (~45-47%), thresholding-only localization gives similarly poor BER (~45-47%, recall pr<0.15), while adding the DeepLab segmentation refinement drops BER to 7.39%-10.13% and raises recall pr to 0.95-0.97. Anti-cropping ablation (Table 7) shows BER with the mechanism stays low (e.g. 1.76%-2.72% at 5% crop, 4.96%-6.86% at 40% crop) versus without it (3.91%-5.90% at 5% crop, rising to ~49-56% at 40% crop). Versus five baselines (Table 9/10): traditional methods (Nakamura et al. 2004, Pramila et al. 2012) show 14-30% BER and cannot survive cropping at all; SSRW (Fang et al. 2019) is competitive under print-and-capture (0.78-2.64% BER in some ranges) but fails under cropping and some angle ranges; DTW (Fang et al. 2021) and TERA (Fang et al. 2022) substantially beat the traditional methods but still trail this paper's method on most conditions, though DTW has lower BER than the proposed method for small crop ratios (gamma<=0.45) due to its codeword-decomposition strategy. On capacity/quality (Table 10), the proposed method embeds 127 bits vs 32-64 bits for baselines, at correspondingly lower PSNR/SSIM (31.77 dB/0.91 vs baselines' 38.97-42.30 dB/0.99) -- an explicit capacity-quality trade-off the authors acknowledge. Runtime (Table 11): embedding 21.55 ms, localization 29.03 ms, extraction 13.43 ms, total 64.01 ms/image (15.62 FPS) on an RTX 2070 Super.
core_contribution: An end-to-end deep-learning framework for screen-shooting-robust image watermarking that jointly trains a U-Net encoder and an STN-based decoder, and contributes: (1) a physically-motivated Moiré-pattern noise simulator (LCD subpixel resampling -> random perspective warp + Gaussian blur -> Bayer CFA demosaicing -> inverse warp) combined with color-gamut, saturation, Gaussian-noise and motion-blur simulation layers used for adversarial-style robustness training against the screen-capture channel; (2) a Just-Noticeable-Distortion (JND) guided perceptual loss (LPIPS distance between the encoder's residual map and a scaled pixel-domain JND coefficient map) that concentrates watermark energy in high-frequency/textured regions to raise visual quality; and (3) two deployment-oriented localization modules -- a DeepLab-V3+-refined foreground segmentation pipeline that rectifies a photographed watermarked region out of a cluttered captured image, and a symmetric (quadruple-embedded, R-channel noise-template) anti-cropping mechanism recoverable via Wiener filtering and column/row symmetry-peak detection -- that let the whole pipeline decode automatically without manual cropping.
threat_model: Not applicable / no adversarial threat model is defined. This is a robustness/signal-processing paper about surviving a benign physical transmission channel (display screen -> camera recapture), not an attacker-vs-defender security paper. The 'attacks' considered (digital cropping, distance/angle/device variation) are treated as channel distortions or, at most, a single generic content-removal operation (cropping) that the anti-cropping module is designed to tolerate -- there is no adaptive adversary, no evasion/poisoning framing, and no analysis of an attacker trying to defeat or forge the watermark.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation: future work is needed for 'watermark decoding from partial screen captures (where only a portion of the displayed image is photographed)' -- the current anti-cropping mechanism only handles digital cropping of the full captured image, not partial physical photographing of a display, and visual quality under higher embedding capacity still needs improvement. Additional limitations evident from the paper's own tables/text: robustness degrades sharply at long capture distance (11.65% BER at 200 cm with the Redmi K30, versus <=3.33% with the Sony A6100 at the same distance), showing strong sensitivity to capture-device sensor quality; cropping ratios beyond ~50-55% cause BER to rise substantially (up to ~14% BER at gamma=0.55) even with the anti-cropping mechanism, and the method requires that at least one full quadrant (25% of the image) survive cropping, so more aggressive or non-edge-aligned crops are not covered; visual quality (31.77 dB PSNR / 0.91 SSIM) is markedly lower than lower-capacity baselines (up to 42.30 dB), an explicit capacity/fidelity trade-off; and DTW beats the proposed method on small-crop BER, indicating the proposed approach is not uniformly best across all conditions.

datasets_used: Flickr1M (Huiskes and Lew, 2008) -- first 100,000 images used for training
Flickr2K (Timofte et al., 2017) -- first 2,000 images used for testing; 500 randomly selected images used for the JND ablation study
quantitative_results: Full method: 127-bit payload, PSNR 30.94 dB, SSIM 0.94, BER 2.86% (NET3 ablation config, Table 4)
SOTA-comparison config: 127-bit payload, PSNR 31.77 dB, SSIM 0.91, LPIPS 0.05 (Table 10)
Standard 40 cm frontal condition: BER 1.09%-1.42% depending on capture device (Table 8)
200 cm distance, Redmi K30: BER 11.65% (worst standard condition, Table 8)
Moire-sim ablation: NET4 (with) vs NET5 (without) BER improvement of 0.10-1.15 percentage points across angles -75 to +75 degrees (Table 5)
Localization ablation: no localization ~45-47% BER; thresholding-only ~45-47% BER, recall pr<0.15; +DeepLab segmentation 7.39%-10.13% BER, recall pr 0.95-0.97 (Table 6)
Anti-cropping ablation: with mechanism 1.76%-14.19% BER across crop ratios 0.05-0.55; without mechanism 3.91%-55.96% BER over the same range (Table 7)
Runtime: embedding 21.55 ms, localization 29.03 ms, extraction 13.43 ms, total 64.01 ms/image = 15.62 FPS on RTX 2070 Super (Table 11)
baselines_compared: Nakamura et al. 2004 (traditional orthogonal-template watermarking): 14-30% BER, cannot survive cropping
Pramila et al. 2012 (traditional): 16-30% BER, cannot survive cropping
SSRW / Fang et al. 2019 (I-SIFT keypoint-based): competitive BER under print-and-capture but fails under cropping and some angles due to Moire-induced SIFT degradation

DTW / Fang et al. 2021 (deep template-based watermarking): beats traditional methods; lower BER than proposed method at small crop ratios (gamma<=0.45) but worse under severe cropping/noise; PSNR 39.73 dB / SSIM 0.99 at 64-bit capacity
TERA / Fang et al. 2022 (screen-to-camera image coding): improved transparency/adaptability but still trails proposed method on most conditions
